title: For the first time in history, England no longer supplies the most new Australians
text:
For the first time in our nation's history, England has been knocked out of top spot when it comes to Australian migration .

New figures from the Australian Bureau of Statistics have revealed that Indian-born residents now make up the largest percentage of﻿ the 8.33 million Australians who were born overseas.

Before 2025, English-born residents sat at number one in front of those born in India, China, New Zealand and the Philippines.

READ MORE: ﻿ Natasha is leaving her 9 to 5 career behind after being made redundant twice

New statistics have shown that Australia's number one overseas country of origin has been surpassed. (Getty)

As of 30 June 2025, the four most common countries of birth were India at 971,020, England at 970,950, China at 732,000 and New Zealand at 638,000.

The data also showed the overseas born population had grown at a faster rate of 3 per cent a year compared to the Australian-born population which grew at an average of 1 per cent a year.

In twenty years from 2005, the percentage of Australian residents born overseas has risen from 24.2 per cent to 32 per cent in 2025.

Proportions have fluctuated during certain eras such as decreased migration during WWI, the Great Depression and WWII.

The new statistics show that 32 per cent of the overall Australian population are people born overseas, meeting the high levels of migration in 1891 towards the end of the gold rush era.

From the 1970s to 2020 there was a stable increase over the years, until 2021 when the COVID-19 pandemic caused travel restrictions, impacting people moving to and from Australia.

Australia's overseas born population has been growing at a faster rate of 3.0% per year. (Getty)

When restrictions were lifted in 2023, the proportion of people born overseas exceeded a 30 per cent increase for the first time since 1893.

The current Australian migration debate centres on reducing high post-pandemic immigration to ease housing pressure, with surveys showing 49 per cent of people favouring such a decrease.

Immigration Minister Tony Burke said in a press conference earlier this month that "post-COVID, we had the sharpest increase".

The Home Affairs Minister said immigration numbers should get to "sustainable levels again." (Alex Ellinghausen)

"And since then, we've been having, with the exception of COVID, the sharpest decrease," he said.

Bourke said the government was making sure numbers got to "sustainable levels again".

But in doing that "you need to make sure that you don't wreck your aged care system".

"You need to make sure that we don't end up with our farmers not being able to get people to pick their fruit," he said.

"All of these things are essential to Australia's economy."

It was part of Burke's strong defence of migration following the Coalition's recent proposal to overhaul Australia's migration policy.

"Australia is and should always be a country where we judge you by who you are, not where you're from."

"People say they love Australia and I do, and almost everybody on this continent does, modern Australia is what they're loving, and we are a multicultural nation.

"Half of our doctors are born overseas, 43 per cent of our registered nurses are born overseas.

43 per cent of our Australian registered nurses are born overseas. (Getty)

"Twenty-eight per cent of people working in building and plumbing trades are born overseas.

"What matters is who you are, not where you're from, and effectively, if we forget the benefits to our nation and to our economy of having a smart tailored migration system, we end up with fewer homes, we would end up with a health system that would collapse.

"We would end up with an age care system that could not provide the care that it needs and that all Australians rely on.

"We are a good country and we should not be setting people against each other."

title: Latham ordered to pay $100k for offensive tweet to MP
text:
Former federal Labor leader Mark Latham has been ordered to pay a fellow state MP the maximum available penalty for vilifying him for his sexuality.

The 65-year-old will be forced to pay Alex Greenwich $100,000 after a tweet directed at him was found to be unlawful sexual harassment .

The sexually explicit tweet from the NSW upper house independent in 2023 has previously been ruled to be defamatory by the Federal Court .

LIVE UPDATES: Trump praises King while continuing complaints about British PM Keir Starmer

Independent MP Mark Latham speaks to media in Camden on Saturday 19th July 2025. Photo: Sitthixay Ditthavong (Sitthixay Ditthavong)

Latham's tweet came in response to a post quoting Greenwich describing him as a "disgusting human being".

The online sparring match between the two politicians followed violent protests outside a church in Sydney's southwest, where Latham was giving a pre-election speech in March 2023.

The NSW Civil and Administrative Tribunal imposed the maximum penalty due to the extreme abuse Greenwich received and the intense impacts he felt.

It found that Greenwich had suffered extreme psychological damage as a result of Latham's actions.

The tribunal did not order Latham to produce an apology, saying it would be pointless given it would likely not be genuine.

Latham was ordered to pay Greenwich's legal costs. (Dominic Lorrimer)

Greenwich welcomed the decision and said the result was a landmark ruling for LGBTQIA+ rights in the state.

"This decision sends a clear message: public figures are not above the law, and online platforms are not a space for unlawful vilification," the member for Sydney said in a statement.

"The tribunal has made clear that the law does not require anyone to endure unlawful vilification or sexual harassment."

Latham was ordered to remove any material from his social media that vilifies Greenwich based on his homosexuality and to refrain from making any more unlawful vilifying statements.

He is also ordered to pay Greenwich's legal costs .

title: Woolworths sales up but supermarket cuts profit forecast amid petrol price spike
text:
Woolworths has reported a significant jump in grocery sales while simultaneously slashing its profit forecasts as the escalating conflict in the Middle East sends shockwaves through the global supply chain.

The retail giant yesterday posted a 4.5 percent increase in group sales over the last quarter to $18.1 billion, with its flagship supermarkets surging by 5.9 percent.

However, the strong result, announced yesterday to the ASX, was overshadowed by a more subdued outlook for the remainder of the financial year, as the retail giant warned surging petrol prices and rising logistics costs were beginning to eat into margins.

READ MORE: Jacinta Price reveals missing girl is her niece

Customers at a check out counter at a Woolworths Group Ltd. grocery store in Sydney, Australia, on Monday, Aug. 21, 2023. Woolworths is scheduled to release earnings results on Aug. 23. Photographer: Brent Lewin/Bloomberg (Bloomberg via Getty Images)

The petrol squeeze

The biggest problem for Woolworths right now is the rising cost of energy.

Because the conflict in the Middle East is making global oil prices jump, the supermarket is getting hit twice: it costs more to run their delivery trucks and stores, and shoppers have less money to spend because they're paying so much more at the petrol pump.

"Looking ahead, the conflict in the Middle East is creating greater uncertainty for our customers, suppliers and team at a time when cost-of-living pressures are already acute," Woolworths chief executive Amanda Bardwell said.

"While the impact on the group to date has been limited, higher fuel costs and secondary effects are likely to have an increasing inflationary impact as we move through the calendar year."

Woolworths warned that these external pressures had forced a downward revision of its profit expectations.

While the retail giant still expects supermarkets to report a rising profit for the full fiancial year, it will no longer be at the upper end of its forecast range.

Shoppers are actually buying more

Despite the cautious profit outlook, Woolworths is seeing a peculiar trend: shoppers are actually buying more items.

Senior markets analyst at Stake, Kylie Purcell, noted that the current growth was being driven by volume rather than just price inflation.

"Woolworths' results highlight a shift," Purcell said.

"Shoppers are becoming more deliberate. They are buying more items per basket and leaning heavily into loyalty programs to stretch their dollar further."

Trust 'the biggest challenge'﻿

The announcement from Woolworths of a sales jump comes as the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) conducts a high-stakes investigation into whether the major supermarkets have been "faking" their discounts while posting multibillion-dollar sales figures as households struggle to pay their grocery bills.

In an effort to protect its market share and manage its reputation during the ACCC investigation, Bardwell today announced a price freeze for shoppers on 300 "household staples".

Sales jumped in the last quarter at Woolworths, but the outlook for the rest of the fiancial year is less optimistic. (Nine)

The freeze, which includes bread, meat and nappies, was a direct response to the "new wave of uncertainty" caused by the Middle East crisis, Bardwell said.

"The conflict has brought a new wave of uncertainty, and we're hearing from our customers that they're worried about what it means for their household budget," she said.

However, the key challenge Woolworths continued to face was rebuilding trust, Purcell said.

"For everyday shoppers already feeling the pinch from higher interest rates and fuel costs, strong sales figures from the supermarket giants are unlikely to win any popularity contests," she said.

"The timing of today's price freeze on 300 staples is telling, but is unlikely to completely ease public annoyance."

title: You're not imagining it, the price of your groceries is soaring
text:
Grocery shopping isn't getting any cheaper for Australians, and new data suggests the worst is yet to come.

With many families now facing weekly grocery bills close to $300, the pressure is mounting: fresh ABS figures show national inflation has surged to 4.6 per cent

According to the Savings.com.au Grocery Price Index, a basket of Coles and Woolies home-brand items averaged $297.95 last month.

Inflation figures suggest that any reprieve for shoppers was short-lived. (Getty)

This represents a $24 increase since early 2024, cementing a painful new baseline for everyday spending.

The inflation trap

Despite grocery-specific inflation having shown signs of easing in March, the 4.6 per cent national spike suggests that any reprieve for shoppers was short-lived.

The challenge for household budgets is that inflation tracks the speed of price increases rather than the actual cost at the register.

In March, the same basket of home-brand items cost $301.95 at Coles and $293.94 at Woolworths. (Getty)

Even if grocery prices rose more slowly in recent months, those hikes were added to a basket that was already at record highs.

With national inflation now surging again, these new increases are being calculated on top of a $300 shop rather than the $250 families were paying two years ago.

The first domino to fall

If you want to know where the rest of the store is headed, look to the dairy aisle. Money expert Joel Gibson says milk costs are often the first domino to fall when supply chain pressures mount.

"We were told that dairy would be the first product category, 'cause of the Iran war, to start going up," he explained on Today . "And it has. So now we have to keep a close eye on the other stuff in the supermarket too."

Milk prices are often the first to rise when supply chain pressures mount. (Supplied)

Supermarkets like Coles, Woolworths, and Aldi have already lifted home-brand milk prices by 20 to 50 cents this month, following calls from farmers facing rising fuel-linked production costs.

Gibson says these "fuel surcharges" are finally starting to flow through the supply chain to shoppers.

Why your weekly total always changes

Even with a strict list, many shoppers find it impossible to predict their final total. Sharp price changes in specific staples, often driven by seasonal shocks or supply chain issues, can swing a bill by $10 or $20 in a single week.

Pink Lady apples shot up 61.5 per cent over the past year. (Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Produce

A Pink Lady 1kg punnet shot up by a national average of 61.5 per cent over the past year. Coles saw an 81.6 per cent jump to $8.90, while Woolworths prices rose a comparatively lower 43.6 per cent to $7.90.

It was a similar story for a 1kg bag of brown onions, which saw an average annual spike of 63.6 per cent in February.

Coles prices went up by 44 per cent, though Woolies shoppers were hit harder with a 89.5 per cent rise for the same bag.

Pantry staples

Eggs have risen 20 per cent in two years due to local supply shocks and bird flu fears. Meanwhile, coffee prices increased by nearly 50 per cent as global weather events contracted supply.

Avian influenza outbreaks in Victoria, New South Wales and the ACT triggered a nationwide egg shortage. (Getty)

When home brands aren't cheap anymore

In the past, families turned to home-brand products to save money. Now, the index shows that even this fallback is no longer offering much relief.

Because the cost of generics has climbed so steeply, a basket of strictly budget items in March 2026 ($297.95) is now more expensive than the index's original baseline was in January 2024 ($273.82).

Even if you buy the absolute cheapest version of every item on your list today, you are still paying $24 more than you would have for a standard shop just two years ago.

The squeeze is even tighter for those relying on convenience. Gibson found that a 2L bottle of milk selling for $3.55 at a regular Woolworths can cost $4.00 at a Woolworths Metro store.

What to expect

Although the frantic price jumps of 2024 have subsided, this week's inflation data confirms that prices won't be dropping back to "normal" anytime soon. Gibson also warned that prices are unlikely to drop once the market settles.

"It never goes down, it just stays the same or goes up more steeply. That's our experience in the past," he said. "In the short term, we are going to unfortunately see big grocery price increases﻿."

title: Natasha is leaving her 9 to 5 career behind after being made redundant twice
text:
The traditional 9 to 5 job is slowly becoming a relic of the pre-COVID workplace .

Job cuts and lay﻿-offs on a local and global scale has spooked many Australian workers into the gig economy, with job loss anxiety hitting a five-year high in April, according to the Westpac-Melbourne Institute Consumer Sentiment Index.

Natasha Ciesielski was made redundant twice in the span of eight years.

She wants to avoid this occupational hazard again by trialling a career away from the traditional salaried job.

Natasha Ciesielski has faced redundancy twice in her career. (Supplied)

Do you have a story? Contact reporter April Glover at april.glover@nine.com.au

The Sydney woman was retrenched for the first time from her job at Canon in 2018.

"I did not stop crying for two weeks, I was devastated. It was a real shock to the system," Ciesielski, 47, told Nine.com.au.

"I'd been out of the job market for a really long time, because I've been headhunted from company to company, so it'd been quite a while since I actually had to apply for anything."

It took a few months to find another role.

The former marketing manager and writer adopted a steely resolve once she found herself back in the job market.

Instead of anxiously waiting for the next round of redundancies, she worked on her side passions, which included writing, studying wine (oenology) and teaching yoga.

"I thought... I'm never putting myself in that position again, in terms not having something else to lean on and putting all my love and my time into my career," she said.

"I was very much a 'career came first' person.

"But I wanted to do it differently.﻿"

Ciesielski pursued her passion of yoga teaching. (Supplied)

The ﻿tap on the shoulder came again earlier this month.

When Ciesielski was made redundant from her senior marking job at American Express, this time, she wasn't as devastated.

She had a casual yoga teaching job and had started her own theatre review website, Bacchus at the Theatre.

Though it was unpredictable, Ciesielski still had some money to fall back on.

"I might only earn $60 a month, or I might earn $300 a month," she said.

"But I'm still getting an income from somewhere else.

"Mentally... ﻿it helped me feel like I had something else."

Ciesielski learned during her first redundancy that there's no shame in asking for help or support.

She immediately took stock of her bills, contacted her internet provider and negotiated a reduced payment fee.

She also cancelled unnecessary subscriptions like Spotify and﻿ spoke to her friends about ways to socialise without spending money.

"I've set things up for myself. I've approached it differently," she said.

"Being older, I have a lot of friends who aren't working too.

"Honestly, it just seems so common, I've had so many friends recently that have been made redundant."

While recovering from the second redundancy blow, ﻿Ciesielski realised she had no desire to jump back into a 9 to 5 job.

If the "dream job" arises, she will take it. But there's no sense of urgency.﻿

"It's more about crafting my dream life, to be honest," Ciesielski added.

"My plan moving forward is to do project and contract work.

"I am loving the time to focus on my interests. I don't want to return to a nine to five at the moment."

Ciesielski learned during her first redundancy that there's no shame in asking for help or support. (Supplied)

'Reframing' your career

Around 268,000 Australians were retrenched in 2025, the highest number since the COVID-19 pandemic ended, according to the Australian Bureau of Statistics.

For many of these workers who have been made redundant, financial pressures like a mortgage or raising children might mean gig work is out of the question.

Chief executive of Dynamic Leadership Programs Australia (DLPA) and Crestcom ANZ Karlie Cremin said job seekers should be open about the type of work they can secure.

"That might mean taking the 'good enough' role for a little bit, because there's very real pressures in business and in the world at the moment that mean sthat good enough might be the best thing on the table for a while," Cremin said.

Cremin shares Ciesielski's ethos of separating your job from your identity.

She advocates her clients to "reflect on and reframe" on what having a career means.

"It can be just a devastating thing and I think the best thing that people can do is kind of take that as a prompt," Cremin said.

"Take the time to reflect on, well, my work is what I do, but it's not who I am – even if it's really, really important to me."

title: The news no Aussie wanted to hear - and it could get worse
text:
Inflation has surged to its highest level in almost three years as borrowers brace for a potential third consecutive interest rate hike to start the year.

New data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics released this morning showed the consumer price index rose 4.6 per cent over the 12 months to March, up from 3.7 per cent in February.

While that was slightly lower than economists' expectations, it remains far higher than the Reserve Bank's target band of 2-3 per cent, and is the worst figure since 2023, when inflation was coming down from a peak of more than 7 per cent.

Surging petrol prices have pushed inflation to its highest level in almost three years. (Peter Rae)

The surge was driven primarily by skyrocketing petrol prices caused by the war in Iran and ensuing closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

According to the ABS, fuel costs rose a whopping 32.8 per cent in March – the highest monthly rise on record.﻿

However, in a silver lining for borrowers fearing another rate hike, the trimmed mean – the RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation – remained steady at 3.3 per cent.﻿

Prior to today's data, financial markets had been pricing in a roughly three-quarters chance of an interest rate rise when the RBA's monetary policy board meets next week, and all big four banks are forecasting a hike.

Nine's Political Editor Charles Croucher said transport costs have been one of the key drivers of an unprecedented rise in monthly inflation.

﻿"This is coming from the bowser, and it's jumping on board the inflation that was already outside the RBA's target band," Croucher said.

"We were already in a bad spot, this has just made it a whole lot worse."

Croucher warned that yet another rate hike at the Reserve Bank's May meeting is "almost inevitable".﻿

Oxford Economics Australia economist Harry McAuley agreed that unwelcome rate announcements are likely to be on the agenda next month.

He said oil price shocks are a central bank's "worst nightmare" and that the RBA has a long road ahead to push the soaring inflation down.﻿

"But the longer the Strait remains closed, the fewer options the board will have; a prolonged closure would force the RBA's hand to hike rates multiple times this year to tame inflation and inflation expectations," McAuley said.

Chalmers said inflation would have been even higher if the excise had not been halved. (Nine)

Inflation will get worse: Chalmers﻿

The inflation figure may increase even further ﻿before it drops again as the Iran war continues, Treasurer Jim Chalmers said.

Chalmers addressed the media following the announcement of a 4.6 per cent inflation﻿ and offered a grim prediction for the next few months.

"This war could drive inflation up even higher before it comes back down again," he said.

"Treasury's expectation is that inflation is likely to peak higher than this, but they are still finalising their forecasts ahead of the budget next month."

But in a small triumph for the government's fuel-saving measures, Chalmers said inflation would have been even higher if the excise had not been halved.

"For the next couple of months, our fuel excise cut has been a very important factor, taking some of the sting out of fuel prices, and that's reflected in the movement in fuel prices," he added.

title: 'Yes, it's sexy': Pauline Hanson gifted $2.1 million new plane by Gina Rinehart
text:
One Nation Leader Pauline Hanson has been gifted a private plane and $2 million ahead of the party's first federal test at next week's Farrer by-election .

She shared the news on her social media this morning, saying it will help her visit more regional towns across the country.

"Yes it was donated. Yes I'm super happy. Yes it's fast. Yes it's amazing. Yes it's going to annoy the Guardian ," she said.

One Nation Leader Pauline Hanson has been gifted a private plane. (X)

"Yes it's a Cirrus G7. Yes it's sexy. Yes I have a pilot.

"No I won't be doing welcome to country each we land. No it's not battery-operated.﻿"

A ﻿spokesperson for Hanson said Cirrus G7 aircraft costs about $2.1 million and was donated by a company owned by Australia's richest person and loyal supporter Gina Rinehart.

One Nation was handed the plane before the announcement today and has already put it to use. ﻿

"Financial donations to the party are used to fund its normal operations and campaign activities," the spokesperson said. ﻿

Hanson also received $1 million from ﻿stockbroker Angus Aitken and wife Sarah and $500,000 each from former Northern Territory chief minister Adam Giles and geologist and climate sceptic Ian Plimer.

Giles and Plimer are employed at Hancock Prospecting, which is owned by Rinehart.

Hanson has publicly declared that Hancock Prospecting has provided her with flights several times in 2025 and 2026.﻿

Hanson said the "faith and investment" in One Nation would be an "enormous help" for their federal campaign. ﻿

"We have a lot of additional fundraising to undertake between now and the cut-off in December to combat the uni-party ahead of the 2028 federal election," she said.

Gina Rinehart is the country's richest person. (Getty)

"I want to pass on my sincere thanks to everyone who has made a contribution to One Nation over the years. I cannot thank you enough for the hope and encouragement you give me to help turn this country around."

One Nation will need to publicly declare the estimated $4 million in donations at the end of this year.

New rules that will cap how much politicians can receive in donations will come into effect around the end of the year. ﻿

One Nation's record rise in popularity in the polls will be tested at a federal level in the Farrer byelection on May 9.

Former opposition leader Sussan Ley held the regional NSW seat for 25 years and left it empty when she retired from politics earlier this year.

One Nation's candidate David Farley has emerged as a real contender for Farrer and is in a close race with independent candidate Michelle Milthorpe.﻿

title: Fed-up residents 'leaving town' due to murky tap water
text:
Polly Rombola reckons water quality has been a hot topic of conversation ever since she moved to the southern New South Wales town of Narrandera about 24 years ago.

When the bore water the town relies on was contaminated by floods in 2022, the situation got worse.

Four years later, nothing has changed.

"People are leaving town because of this," Ms Rombola said.

"You can smell it on your skin, even if you use scented soap, it smells, and you're scratching all the time.

" [Clean water] is a basic human right. "

Polly Rombola is a long-time resident of Narrandera. (ABC Riverina: Rachel Holdsworth. )

According to the Narrandera Shire Council's website, the discolouration occurs when iron and manganese sediment in the pipes or water mains is stirred up.

The site says tap water is tested every week "for a vast range of chemical and microbiological particles, ensuring it is safe to drink".

The site also offers advice for those whose clothes are stained by discoloured water.

It says staining caused by iron and manganese is "best removed by an acid-based cleaning agent. Several commercial rust removers work quite well and can be purchased in local supermarkets and hardware stores".

Polly Rombola says she has to test the water before doing her washing in case it stains her whites. (Supplied: Polly Rombola )

Local cafe owner Julie Pearson said the water would typically get to "murky" levels a few times a month.

"It looks like we have just scooped it out of the Murrumbidgee River," Ms Pearson said.

"You can't drink it.

"We have a filter that we fill our kettle with, and we buy bottled water, which has doubled in price over the last couple of months."

Julie Pearson says the town's water issues impact her business and personal life. (ABC Riverina: Nicola Ceccato. )

Ms Pearson said the appliances she used in both her personal and business life were damaged by the water.

"Irons, washing machines, anything that takes water … it corrodes inside and rusts them out really quickly," she said.

Neville Kschenka hopes the Farrer by-election will spark some action for the town's decades-long water quality crisis. (ABC Riverina: Nicola Ceccato)

Narrandera Mayor Neville Kschenka said the decades-long water quality crisis was getting worse, but the council could not afford a treatment plant to fix the problem.

"Residents are continually asking us what we can do about it, and quite frankly, [we need] about $32 million, and our rate payers just can't afford that," he said.

"We're looking for support from both levels of government so we can have a water treatment plant."

The federal Coalition recently pledged $16 million to support construction of a water treatment plant if it is elected at the 2028 election, but an equivalent state contribution would still be needed.

'An interim solution'

Cr Kschenka said the council had done its best to alleviate the water quality issues.

"We've paid plumbers to install around 450 filters for residents, and we continue to flush the main, but that all costs money, and they're only temporary measures," he said.

The NSW government said last year it would provide $1 million to complete a business case for a water treatment plant, and almost $215,000 to carry out preliminary investigations.

The state's water minister, Rose Jackson, said at the time that the government was "in direct contact with council to get a better understanding of what an interim solution could look like".

"We are continuing to provide expert advice and technical support to ensure the community is supplied with water that meets Australian Drinking Water Guidelines."

Steph Cooke says there needs to be an "interim solution" for the town's water crisis, as a funding push continues for a water treatment plant. (ABC Riverina: Rachel Holdsworth. )

But NSW Nationals Member for Cootamundra Steph Cooke said the community should be guaranteed funding to end the town's ongoing water quality issues, and some temporary relief in the meantime.

"Now we are at a critical moment where we need to secure the $30 to $32 million to build this plant that this community so desperately needs," she said.

"Fund the temporary measures that we have been calling for over 18 months, a temporary water treatment plant to deal with it in the interim until the permanent one is built."

Polly Rombola has documented the discoloured water since she moved to town over two decades ago. (Supplied: Polly Rombola)

Cr Kschenka said a town that provided "food, fibre and wealth" for the nation deserved cleaner drinking water.

"We were the town that started irrigation and created all those communities further down the river," he said.

title: Woolworths discounts not 'inherently misleading', judge says
text:
There have been tense exchanges in the Federal Court, with the judge overseeing the bombshell case between the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) and Woolworths questioning the consumer watchdog's case.

The day began with the ACCC's legal counsel Michael Hodge KC giving closing remarks, but it was not long before Justice Michael O'Bryan intervened.

Woolworths has been accused of misleading shoppers with fake discounts on more than 260 items that were sold under its Prices Dropped promotions.

The ACCC claimed shoppers thought they were saving money on items being sold on Prices Dropped tickets, when they were actually paying more than the previous regular price.

During the two weeks of hearings, the court has heard evidence about Woolworths breaching its internal guidelines over the prices charged for Fab laundry powder and Oreo biscuits.

The ACCC has accused Woolworths of misleading customers by using fake discounts as part of its "Prices Dropped" marketing program. (ABC News: Billy Cooper)

Shortly after today's evidence began, Justice O'Bryan told Mr Hodge the Prices Dropped scheme itself did not appear to be deliberately misleading.

"Within that whole scheme, if I can call it that, promotional strategy, whatever you'd like to call it, none of it strikes me as nefarious or inherently misleading."

Justice O'Bryan then asked Mr Hodge whether he should decide the case on other key factors.

"Is this price legitimately established in the market?"

"Doesn't it really come back to factors such as the genuineness of the price, how the price was chosen, the period over which it is offered, those sorts of matters?"

Mr Hodge maintained the judge should find that consumers had clearly been misled.

"Woolworths is representing to consumers, 'you … are going to pay less for this product as the new regular shelf price … compared to what was the previous regular of price' and that is the defining feature by itself. That means that the representation, in this case, is misleading."

Mr Hodge conceded that if Woolworths had taken longer to genuinely establish the higher prices before discounting the products, the consumer watchdog would never have sued the supermarket giant.

"We would not be here if the price establishment periods had lasted for six months."

Judge accidentally names wrong supermarket

There have also been some lighthearted moments today.

The ACCC has also sued Coles over similar allegations about illusionary discounts — a case that Justice O'Bryan has reserved his judgement on.

The hearings for the Coles case concluded in February.

Today, Justice O'Bryan repeatedly referred to Coles by mistake when he meant to say Woolworths.

"I pick myself up, I apologise for doing that," he said, laughing.

The price patterns that landed Coles in court The pricing patterns of these six products show how Coles stands accused of using its Down Down marketing campaign to dupe shoppers.

Woolworths has rejected the allegations and argued the higher "was" price shown on each Prices Dropped ticket was a legitimate price.

That was, the supermarket giant said, because the products were sold at substantial volumes, and in most cases, the earlier price was no longer relevant because the supplier had requested a price increase.

It maintains that for each product the period it charged the higher price was reasonable, making it a legitimate discount.

That was an area Justice O'Bryan also probed.

He questioned whether he should take into account that Woolworths was receiving these requests from suppliers to increase the price.

"This wasn't just an arbitrary spiking of a price, there was a legitimate negotiation going on. There were cost price increases," Justice O'Bryan said.

Woolworths is the largest player in the grocery sector with more than 1,100 supermarkets and Metro stores nationally. (ABC News: Billy Cooper)

In response, Mr Hodge said cost increases had no relevance to whether consumers had been misled by the Prices Dropped ticket because the case was about what people were paying for the products compared to what they had been previously paying.

Justice O'Bryan also pushed back on the ACCC's representation about the higher "was" price and what it said about Woolworths' conduct.

He put to Mr Hodge the higher price had been selected based on the supplier's recommended retail price and had not been "spiked arbitrarily."

"[It] wasn't plucked out of the air," Justice O'Bryan said.

Mr Hodge challenged that assertion, saying the evidence largely showed Woolworths had selected those prices to be a higher "was" price not for an ongoing price in the market.

After Mr Hodge said he had finished addressing Justice O'Bryan's queries, the judge offered a lighthearted response.

"Thank you, I've taken you way off course, Mr Hodge. You can go back to paragraph one [of your closing remarks] now," he joked.

The hearing continues.

title: Wine code of conduct details emerge as industry continues to shrink
text:
Winemakers must publish payment terms and have their payment practices monitored under a new mandatory code of conduct for the wine industry.

Announced late last year, the federal government has now released details of the code, which will come into effect on January 1, 2027.

The incoming code will apply automatically to all wine grape buyers, but only when a grower has a contract with a winemaker.

It will replace the current voluntary code of conduct, which wineries can opt into.

Under the code, growers will also be able to make anonymous complaints about wine companies.

Wine industry growers and representatives have welcomed the new code, but said it was missing support measures for aging growers unable to afford to diversify, leave the industry, or resolve the oversupply.

Jason Perrin says the code of conduct will improve behaviour. (ABC News: Eliza Berlage)

South Australian grapegrower Jason Perrin said having access to minimum prices would let growers make informed decisions for their business.

"We've seen the consequences when they don't, one winery released prices in early November, then cut them significantly in late December and again in January," Mr Perrin said.

"Growers made decisions based on the initial figures, like leasing expensive water, only to be caught out later. That can't continue.

"Without early pricing, growers are forced to guess. Water allocations are already low, and lease prices are likely to rise.

" Growers need certainty to make decisions. "

Jason Perrin says the industry is struggling with growers unable to pay water bills. (ABC News: Eliza Berlage)

The federal government said the mandatory code would make agreements between growers and winemakers more transparent and binding.

"Winemakers should make earlier, binding offers for each grape variety they seek under contract … and these offer prices should be made public," a government spokesman said.

Contract conditional

In Victoria, Murray Valley Winegrowers chair Chris Dent did not expect the code to influence next year's vintage.

"With the code only coming into effect on 1 January, we'll miss those key timing windows for the next vintage, so the full benefits probably won't be realised until the 2028 vintage," he said.

"For the upcoming vintage, we're unlikely to see much benefit beyond things like dispute resolution mechanisms and other details that are still being worked out."

Chris Dent says some growers are already facing difficult decisions. (Supplied: Chris Dent)

Mr Dent said most Sunraysia grapegrowers would not benefit from the code of conduct as they did not have contracts with the wineries.

"In the Murray Valley, only about 40 per cent of growers are on long-term contracts … that gives an idea of who will benefit early on," Mr Dent said.

"Over time, uncontracted growers may also benefit once they secure buyers and enter contracts, but initially, the advantages will be limited to those already contracted.

"The code isn't going to solve all the industry's problems anyway.

"The challenges are much broader than just grower–winery relationships. We're dealing with supply and demand issues, and a structural oversupply that needs to be addressed."

title: Why the latest inflation data matters to you
text:
Hi, I'm ABC business journalist Emily Stewart, and I'm here to help you understand the latest inflation figures, which were released yesterday. 🤓

It can be a worrying time as we see the effects of the war in Iran playing out here in Australia. I want to help you better understand the key moments in our economy that could affect your personal finances so you can make more informed decisions for you and your household.

If you have a question for me, please reach out. Thanks to everyone who sent questions earlier in the week. I have some answers below.

What is inflation?

Inflation is the rate at which the prices of goods and services increase, or inflate, over time. 📈

The Australian Bureau of Statistics collects information every month on a "basket" of products and services, including housing, food and education.

It then reports back on how much prices have gone up (or down). The report is the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which measures inflation.

The CPI tracks how much we pay for a hypothetical "basket" of goods and services. (ABC News: Jarrod Fankhauser)

Why is everyone talking about it?

What happened: The latest CPI data was released yesterday.

What it showed: The CPI rose 4.6 per cent in the 12 months to March 2026, the highest since September 2023, when Australia's economy was rebounding after the COVID-19 pandemic. 🤒

Why did this happen? Inflation was already higher than the RBA would like before the Iran war broke out. But the increase in petrol prices since the end of February has been an important reason for the rising CPI figure.

Why is it important? Inflation affects all of us, making the things we buy and the services we pay for more expensive. So if you've been feeling like your money isn't going as far as it used to, this data confirms your fears.

See how the price of everything has changed With the rate of inflation rising this week, we've tracked how the price of everyday items has increased.

Another reason it is important is because the decision-makers at the Reserve Bank will use the CPI data to help them decide whether to raise interest rates next week. And higher interest rates mean people with a mortgage will pay more on their repayments. 💸🏠

Should you be worried?

I think it's worth being prepared for the fact that prices are likely to continue rising at a pretty high rate.

This month's headline CPI figure is actually slightly lower than what many economists were expecting.

However, the consensus is that inflation will continue to rise beyond 5 per cent over the coming months because the high price of fuel is flowing through to areas like housing, food and services.

When it comes to what the Reserve Bank will do next week, things are less clear than a few days ago.

The bank's preferred measure of inflation, underlying or trimmed mean inflation, was steady at 3.3 per cent, but still higher than what the central bank is aiming for (2.5 per cent).

Before the figures came out, financial markets were almost certain interest rates would rise next week.

However, that's been scaled back. There are signs that domestic price pressures (not including fuel) are easing somewhat, and there are some arguments that rates could actually stay on hold. However, most economists are still expecting a rate rise on Tuesday.

What comes next?

What: The Reserve Bank will announce whether or not interest rates will change.

When: Tuesday, May 5 at 2:30pm AEST ⏰

Your questions answered

Alex asks: The CPI and trimmed mean were at their highest peaks in 2023. So why are things so expensive now when the CPI and trimmed mean have dropped to the 3-4 per cent range?

Emily: That's because CPI actually measures the rate of price change, not the price level. So prices are still going up, from that higher base, just by slightly less than what they were a few years ago. The RBA wants prices to rise in the 2-3 per cent range.

Malcolm asks: Why are people with bank loans asked to fight inflation when people without loans are not? Why doesn't the government introduce measures that ensure everyone fights inflation?

Emily: You're right, raising the official cash rate is often called a "blunt tool", because it impacts the entire economy rather than specific sources of inflation. By lifting interest rates, it aims to slow down spending, but that means it has a faster and typically larger impact on households with a mortgage. When central banks implement these types of measures, it's called monetary policy. As you suggest, another way to influence the economy is fiscal policy, so the government could reduce its spending and increase taxes. However, that is politically difficult, so in recent decades most governments tend to leave the heavy lifting up to central banks.

Lowering inflation without rate rises Price caps on gas and energy bill subsidies may be lowering inflation. Can we do more things like that, instead of just raising interest rates?

Pat and Russell ask: With fuel prices already limiting the capacity of spending for a lot of households, why does the Reserve Bank feel the need to double down on the hit and raise interest rates?

Emily: It's a good point. Higher fuel prices do mean many households have been forced to cut their spending in other areas. The RBA will take this in to account when it looks at how much demand there is in the economy, and how high it thinks interest rates need to go. However, inflation is still well above the bank's target, so even though a lot of it is driven by a fuel price shock, the bank will be concerned that it will flow through to rising prices and wage demands more broadly.

That's all I have for you this week. I'll be back next week to help you through the RBA announcement. Follow me for more on personal finance.

Disclaimer: This information is just general in nature. If you need personalised financial advice, please see a professional.

title: Lyon clarifies resignation offer over comment to First Nations players
text:
St Kilda coach Ross Lyon is proud of the way the club reacted after he offended a group of the club's First Nations players.

Lyon conceded he "made an error" and was held to account by his players regarding his remarks during a training session earlier this month.

As three First Nations players linked up with the ball at training, Lyon said: "I love the brother boy connection, but we all have to remember we are part of a bigger team here."

Bradley Hill and Nasiah Wanganeen-Milera were among those who took exception to the comment, with Hill contacting Lyon the following day.

Lyon met with the group and resolved the situation, with the players satisfied their coach had said the wrong thing but had not intended to be racist.

Today, Lyon said there was no formal complaint made to the AFL.

He rejected suggestions Saints chief executive Carl Dilena should have been brought in to handle the issue at the time.

"It was a really great process driven by the players," Lyon said.

Indigenous players defend Lyon Several indigenous players who have played under Saints coach Ross Lyon come to his defence after comments he made during a training session led to a meeting where Lyon offered to stand down.

"It talks to our environment in the way it was handled, and the shop floor took care of it.

"If there had been an impasse and a complaint, it would have been taken outside of that office and escalated.

"Those that are alluding to, 'Why didn't it go to the CEO?' it's because it became a non-issue that was resolved really quickly and appropriately."

Hill and Wanganeen-Milera have shown public support for Lyon since the issue was first made public through a Seven Network report on Monday night.

It was reported Lyon offered his resignation at the time of the incident.

But today he moved to clarify that situation.

"All relationships are built on trust … so the context of that was, 'If you don't think I can rebuild the trust, I would consider my position,'" Lyon said.

The coach said he "hugged it out" with the First Nations players at their meeting.

"I am grateful that we got together. It's a psychologically safe environment, we're all accountable," Lyon said.

"Everyone talks about psychological safety and the ability to be your authentic self, express yourself and your opinion, and be safe about it and be heard.

"That's what occurred, and we're really proud of that."

St Kilda next plays Carlton at Docklands on Saturday night.

AAP

title: 20 years after the original, The Devil Wears Prada 2 has something to say
text:
Besieged by scandal, squeezed by budget cuts and reined in by HR, Miranda Priestly is not quite the same evil genius who lorded over The Devil Wears Prada.

Fast facts about The Devil Wears Prada 2 What: After a series of contrived events, Andy Sachs returns to work at Runway Magazine Starring: Anne Hathaway, Meryl Streep, Stanley Tucci, Emily Blunt Directed by: David Frankel When: In cinemas now Likely to make you feel: Nostalgic

Runway Magazine, just like its real-life counterparts in cultural journalism, teeters on a dangerous precipice. Forget haute couture — it's the fleece Patagonia vest of corporate terrorists that reign supreme.

Though she may not have entirely lost her sadistic streak, Streep's ruthless editor-in-chief now re-emerges into the Zeitgeist as an unlikely hero.

It's hard not to be grateful that someone still has standards.

It goes without saying there's no good reason for this sequel to exist — but in fairness, it's not like we often get to appreciate the well-honed pleasures of a mid-budget studio movie these days.

The existential crisis facing Runway has also seeped into Hollywood, where studio execs have long ceased to put effort into original chick flicks. The Devil Wears Prada 2 takes pains to prove its relevance within this era of cultural decline, but only succeeds as a cosy reunion tour.

After 15 years spent reporting across the globe, eternal go-getter Andy Sachs (Anne Hathaway) finds herself back in New York and out of a job when her news organisation is shuttered for a write-down.

Meanwhile, Runway has recently come under fire after unwittingly providing cover for a notorious sweatshop. (In this alternate reality, maybe the SHEIN influencer tours were a roaring success.)

In a Sorkin-esque turn of events, Andy makes an impassioned plea on behalf of her dying industry that goes viral and grabs the attention of her former employer, who offers her a job as a feature writer to help restore the magazine's perception of credibility.

Andy (Anne Hathaway) and Miranda (Meryl Streep) are pushed back into each others' orbits thanks to the crumbling journalism industry. (Supplied: 20th Century Studios)

Andy takes the opportunity, though not without an ulterior backup plan of penning a biography about her legendarily demanding boss. And so, after decades of striking out on her own, our plucky protagonist is more or less back where she started.

In weaving a new story from old fabric, The Devil Wears Prada 2 necessarily has to undo the bravest choice of its predecessor: Andy's refusal to sign her soul away to Runway.

It was a fitting culmination of a fine balancing act, in which the fashion world and its difficult women were given their due, but made for an uneasy fit for a journalist who made her name writing about a janitor's union. The Andy we last knew was horrified at the thought of becoming a Miranda.

The original's rich subtext about the cult-like hierarchy of the industry and the self-effacement of buying into it has largely been hand-waved away because, well, who has integrity to spare anymore?

Returning director David Frankel (Inventing Anna) and screenwriter Aline Brosh McKenna (Crazy Ex-Girlfriend) nonetheless retain a grasp on the frothy pleasures of this material.

A terrible work-life balance can make for entertaining cinema, especially when it involves a spirited Anne Hathaway doggedly chasing leads and tearing across New York, limbs flailing, all while the camera whips around and crash-zooms in pursuit.

The Devil Wears Prada 2 reunites the Runway gang from 20 years ago. (Supplied: 20th Century Studios)

The supporting cast remains enjoyable company. Stanley Tucci (Conclave) returns as fashion director Nigel, noticeably less camp but more endearingly paternal as Andy's work bestie. Emily (Emily Blunt, Oppenheimer) now has a senior role at Dior and a billionaire boyfriend (Justin Theroux), and relishes the opportunity to play hardball with Miranda while Runway weathers its rough patch.

In the absence of her (excessively, if I'm being honest) maligned chef boyfriend from the last film, Andy now has free reign to pursue another Australian love interest, swapping out Simon Baker (Boy Swallows Universe) for a more down-to-earth and authentically accented Patrick Brammall (Colin from Accounts).

Just as the film gets into the groove of Andy’s new lifestyle, the story becomes overshadowed by the mounting backdrop of cutthroat corporate dealings, the stakes aligning more with the billion-dollar power plays of Succession (even down to a picturesque detour to Lake Como).

Andy's foil Emily (Emily Blunt) returns but now she's a bigwig at Dior. (Supplied: 20th Century Studios)

As in the previous film, Meryl Streep anchors the proceedings with gravitas and piercing wit.

It's notably the first time she's sunk her teeth into a sequel (five minutes in Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again doesn't count, sorry), and — with significantly more of the film taking place from her POV — she takes the chance to round out the character with a more melancholy, human presence.

But where The Devil Wears Prada provocatively explored the humiliations we'd endure for the promise of a dream job, its sequel is overly keen to humanise Miranda Priestly.

A well-earned redemption arc can be a joy; the eagerness of sequels (including anything from Terminator 2 to every Fast and Furious movie) to nullify the indomitable power of its villains between instalments is a plague.

Which isn't to say it isn't fun to watch Miranda struggle with the ordeal of hanging up her own coats, suffer through the indignities of economy class, or loosen up over a bottle of Rose at her home in the Hamptons. In one of the film's more disarming moments, we see her chewed out by her own boss over the phone.

But it all smacks of image rehabilitation for a real-life figure who, frankly, doesn't need it.

We love Miranda for her unlikeable qualities.

The original film resonated because — as much as it rekindled traumatic workplace memories — it also captured the allure of being an uncompromising woman who could terraform the fashion world in between parent-teacher interviews.

Similarly, Vogue's former editor-in-chief Anna Wintour, the most prominent inspiration for the character, has endured as a cultural icon by embodying the brilliance and the tyranny of the girl-boss supreme.

The Devil Wears Prada 2 might humanise Miranda but she still looks fabulous. (Supplied: 20th Century Studios)

The excessive fat jokes in the original film came downstream of the beauty standards cultivated by her reign, in which models were forced to slim down before gracing the pages of Vogue. Wintour has personally apologised in recent years for the lack of diversity in staff and talent at the magazine; close collaborator André Leon Talley is on record about the "huge emotional and psychological scars" endured from their relationship.

Enjoyable as the film will be to returning fans, you can't help but feel like the devil hasn't been given her due.

The Devil Wears Prada 2 is in cinemas now.

title: ASIC drops case against Liberty Bell Bay smelter as buyer sought
text:
The corporate regulator has dropped its case against Australia's only manganese smelter, Liberty Bell Bay, for failing to file its historical financial accounts, as administrators battle to keep the workforce intact and sell the company.

The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) moved to wind up Liberty Bell Bay, based in northern Tasmania, in the New South Wales Supreme Court in March.

ASIC alleged the company's former owner, British businessman Sanjeev Gupta's GFG Alliance, had failed to file annual financial reports from the 2021 to 2025 fiscal years.

ASIC and administrators Ernst and Young yesterday reached an agreement, approved by the Supreme Court, to discontinue the proceedings altogether and further hearings have been vacated.

Redundancies were avoided earlier this month due to a loan from the state and federal governments. (ABC News: Kelsey Reid)

Operations at the Liberty Bell Bay plant shut down last May after GFG Alliance collapsed. Since that time the plant has been under care and maintenance and the 216-strong workforce has been reporting for duty and continuing to get paid while potential buyers are sought.

Earlier this month, administrators announced there was no longer enough money to continue to pay the entire workforce and about 175 workers would need to take leave without pay or face redundancy.

But workers got a lifeline last week when Tasmanian Premier Jeremy Rockliff and Federal Industry Minister Tim Ayres confirmed a $3 million loan split 50:50 would be provided to Ernst and Young to support workers at the critical manufacturing facility.

"We're backing the workers, their families and the community through this sale process because this facility and these jobs matter," Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a statement.

The ABC understands administrators have welcomed the discontinuance of ASIC's proceedings as it removes a potential obstacle to the sale of Liberty Bell Bay.

title: Portrait of Jacob Collins wins Archibald Packing Room Prize
text:
Sean Layh has won the $3,000 Archibald Packing Room Prize for his portrait of British Australian actor Jacob Collins, The tragicall historie of Hamlet, Prince of Denmarke.

Layh first met his subject after he saw him perform as Hamlet in Melbourne in 2024.

"It was performed by candlelight in a tight physical setting and blended Hamlet's dark introspection with the plot's spooky supernatural undercurrent," the Melbourne-based artist said in his artist statement.

In his acceptance speech, Layh added that he left the theatre "possessed with the idea of doing something with that".

" This painting is a painting of Jacob in his craft, portraying one of Western literature and theatre's most mercurial characters in Hamlet. "

A self-taught artist, inspired by classical literature and theatre, Layh asked Collins to pose for the Archibald as Hamlet, initially suggesting he paint the Danish prince's death scene.

By leaving out props like swords and poisoned chalices, Layh and Collins found that the final work "captured more of the character's melancholy and inability to act than his final moments", Layh explained in his artist statement.

"Compositionally, the painting references Albert Maignan's The last moments of Chlodobert, which hangs at the National Gallery of Victoria."

Layh's Shakespeare-inspired portrait of Collins, The tragicall historie of Hamlet, Prince of Denmarke. (Supplied: AGNSW/Jenni Carter)

Accepting the award at the Art Gallery of New South Wales (AGNSW), Layh described his win as "one of the great honours of my professional life".

The portrait becomes the ninth painting of an actor to take out the Packing Room Prize, alongside works like Meyne Wyatt's 2020 self-portrait and Tessa MacKay's painting of David Wenham from 2019.

It is one of 59 finalists for the prize, from 1034 entries, alongside paintings of the likes of Governor-General Sam Mostyn, Bondi hero Ahmed Al Ahmed, AFL player Mitch Brown and ABC journalist Virginia Trioli (Creative Types).

Julia Dover's portrait of Bondi terror attack hero Ahmed Al Ahmed, My friend. (Supplied: AGNSW/Jenni Carter)

Jump to the full list of Archibald finalists

What the Archibald means

When Layh was 18 in 2001, he went to see the Archibald, where he encountered Paul Newton's painting of Roy and HG (aka John Doyle and Greig Pickhaver).

"[That painting] really instructed me on what representational painting could be for an artist like myself, who has a bit more of a traditional bent to his work," Layh said in his acceptance speech.

In 2001, Newton's painting won both People's Choice and the Packing Room Prize.

"To stand here a quarter of a century later and actually take the same award is deeply satisfying," Layh said.

What is the Packing Room Prize?

Since 1991, the Packing Room Prize, worth $3,000, has been chosen by the Art Gallery of NSW staff who handle the portraits in the gallery's packing room.

Past winners of the Packing Room Prize include portraits of artist Jason Phu, musician Baker Boy, late comedian Cal Wilson and filmmaker Taika Waititi. No artist who has won the Packing Room Prize has ever gone on to win the Archibald Prize.

Who are the finalists for the Archibald Prize?

Five past winners of the Archibald Prize are finalists in 2026: Peter Wegner, who won the 100th edition in 2021; Vincent Namatjira, the first Indigenous artist to win, in 2020; 2018 winner Yvette Coppersmith; 2017 winner Mitch Cairns; and 2009 winner Guido Maestri.

If one of these artists wins on May 8, they'll join 16 others who have won twice or more, including Del Kathryn Barton.

Twenty-five works in this year's Archibald are first-time finalists, up against a number of veterans, including 13-time finalist Tom Carment, and 12-time finalists Natasha Bieniek and Tsering Hannaford.

All three veterans have painted artists, with Carment and Bieniek entering self-portraits, while Hannaford painted last year's People's Choice winner Loribelle Spirovski. The featured works include paintings of 19 artists in all; of those five are self-portraits.

Portrait of William Barton wins Archibald People's Choice Loribelle Spirovski has won the Archibald Prize People's Choice Award for a portrait of Kalkadunga musician William Barton she painted with her fingers.

Spirovski is a finalist again this year, with a portrait, painted with her fingers, of musician Daniel Johns.

The Silverchair frontman is one of six musicians painted for this year's Archibald, alongside Adam Hyde (aka Keli Holiday) and Cody Simpson.

They're joined by five paintings of fashion designers, including Akira Isogawa and Romance Was Born's Anna Plunkett; and eight actors including Marta Dusseldorp (ABC TV's Bay of Fires), Phoebe Tonkin (Boy Swallows Universe) and two Heartbreak High stars: Chloé Hayden and Gemma Chua-Tran.

Loribelle Spirovski's portrait of musician Daniel Johns, Fingerpainting of Daniel Johns. (Supplied: AGNSW/Jenni Carter)

Other recognisable faces on the gallery walls include artist Khaled Sabsabi, who was controversially dumped then reinstated as Australia's representative at the Venice Biennale (which opens next week), and journalists Jan Fran and Jessica Rowe.

Of the 63 people painted across the 59 finalist works, 32 are men and 31 are women, and six are First Nations. It's the first year since 2022 works by male artists outnumber those by women, at 33 to 26.

Finalists for the Archibald Prize are chosen by the board of trustees of the AGNSW, including artists Tony Albert and Caroline Rothwell.

Stieg Persson's portrait of ABC broadcaster Virginia Trioli, Virginia and Cora. (Supplied: Art Gallery of New South Wales/Jenni Carter)

The Wynne and Sulman prizes

The finalists for the $50,000 Wynne Prize for landscape painting or figure sculpture, and the $40,000 Sir John Sulman Prize for a genre or subject painting or mural were also announced on Thursday.

This year's Sulman is judged by artist Del Kathryn Barton, herself a two-time winner of the Archibald and former finalist in both the Wynne and the Sulman.

Warlpiri artist Adrian Jangala Robertson is a finalist in the Archibald, Wynne and Sulman prizes. Tom Carment, Robert Fielding, Marikit Santiago and Natasha Walsh are up for both the Archibald and the Wynne, while Karen Black, Gunybi Ganambarr and Noel McKenna are up for the Wynne and the Sulman.

Wynne Prize finalist Gunybi Ganambarr's painting of Gunyuru/Round Hat Island, Garrapara. (Supplied: AGNSW/Diana Panuccio)

Among the well-known artists in the Wynne is 91-year-old artist Elisabeth Cummings, who Peter Wegner painted for the Archibald, and Pitjantjatjara artist Iluwanti Ken, who Richard Lewer painted for the Archibald.

Among the 26 finalists in the Sulman Prize are Joan Ross, a Scotland-born artist who won the Sulman in 2017, and Suzanne Archer, who won the Wynne in 1994.
